title: How to Get a Real Estate License in Michigan
excerpt: Starting your real estate career in Michigan might seem challenging, but with the right guidance, it’s a clear and manageable process. This detailed guide will…
content:
Starting your real estate career in Michigan might seem challenging, but with the right guidance, it’s a clear and manageable process. This detailed guide will take you through each necessary step, ensuring you’re fully prepared to launch your real estate career in Michigan. Let’s begin your journey to becoming a licensed real estate agent.

Key Requirements at a Glance

Minimum Age: 18 | Pre-Licensing Education: 40 hours | Exam: 100 questions in 3.5 hours, 75% to pass

Table of Contents

  1. Basic Requirements
  2. Choosing a Real Estate School
  3. Find a Sponsoring Broker
  4. Why Join Smart Agent Alliance?

Estimated Costs

Exam Fee: $79/attempt | Application Fee: $88 | Fingerprinting: $10-$77

Basic Requirements

Before getting into the specifics, it’s crucial to grasp the basic requirements for obtaining a real estate license in Michigan. These are the essential criteria you need to meet:

To apply for a real estate license in Michigan, applicants must be at least 18 years old. This ensures that individuals have reached the required level of maturity and legal responsibility needed for the profession.

You do not need to be a Michigan resident to apply for a real estate license, but you must provide a valid Social Security number. This is essential for conducting background checks and ensuring compliance with state regulations. Non-residents must meet the same standards as Michigan residents.

Integrity is critical in the real estate profession. The Michigan Department of Licensing and Regulatory Affairs (LARA) will conduct a comprehensive background check. Any criminal convictions could lead to the denial of your license. Being transparent about any past legal issues and showing evidence of rehabilitation can strengthen your application.

In Michigan, you must complete 40 hours of pre-licensing education. This course covers crucial topics such as real estate principles, practices, and Michigan-specific laws.

The required course to get your Michigan license includes 4 hours on federal and state civil rights laws, ensuring comprehensive legal knowledge. Additionally, 36 hours cover essential real estate topics like finance, agency laws, and property transactions, preparing you for all aspects of the profession.

This category fulfills the state-mandated requirement by dividing the 4 hours evenly between federal and Michigan-specific civil rights laws. The federal portion covers major laws like the Civil Rights Acts, Fair Housing Act, and Equal Credit Opportunity Act, as well as issues like redlining and steering. The Michigan portion addresses state-specific laws such as the Elliott-Larsen Civil Rights Act, the Persons with Disabilities Civil Rights Act, and details on filing Fair Housing complaints.

This category comprises 36 hours of instruction across 16 levels, covering essential real estate topics. These include foundational knowledge of real estate, principles of finance, agency laws, property condition disclosures, purchase contracts, and the processes of closing and escrow. Each level varies in length depending on the complexity of the subject.

Choosing a Real Estate School

Selecting the right real estate school is crucial. Look for accredited schools that offer flexibility, especially if you have a busy schedule. Online courses can provide the convenience of studying at your own pace.

Research Schools

Start by researching various real estate schools and reading reviews from past students. Look for schools with high pass rates for the real estate exam and positive feedback on their teaching methods.

Online vs In-Person Classes

Decide whether you prefer online or in-person classes. Online courses offer flexibility and convenience, allowing you to study at your own pace. In-person classes provide direct interaction with instructors and peers, which can enhance your learning experience.

Evaluate Course Materials

Evaluate the course materials and support services provided by the school. High-quality textbooks, interactive lessons, and access to instructors for questions can significantly impact your success. Some schools also offer additional resources like exam prep courses and study groups.

Pro Tip

Start studying early and take practice exams regularly. Most candidates who fail do so because they rush through the pre-licensing coursework. Give yourself enough time to truly understand the material, not just memorize it.

Study Tips

Create a daily study schedule that covers all exam topics, ensuring regular review sessions. (Asana provides a great FREE task manager)

Utilize flashcards for key terms and concepts to enhance memorization. (These Quizlet Flashcards may be helpful)

Regularly take practice tests to gauge your understanding and get familiar with the exam format. (Free Florida Real Estate Practice Exam)

After completing your pre-licensing coursework, you’ll need to submit your license application to the Michigan Department of Licensing and Regulatory Affairs (LARA).

Complete the Application Form: Fill out the online application form available on the LARA website.

Gather Required Documents: Ensure you have proof of completed pre-licensing education and necessary identification documents ready to submit.

Submit Your Application: Submit your application and required documents online through the LARA online services portal.

Complete the Application Form: Fill out the online application form available on the LARA website.

The DBPR will take several weeks to process your application. During this time, they will review your transcripts and verify that you meet all the requirements. Make sure to double-check your application for completeness to avoid any delays.

You can track the status of your application online through the DBPR’s website. Regularly checking the status can help you stay informed about any issues or additional requirements. If there are delays, contacting the DBPR directly can provide clarity and expedite the process.

The exam is a 3.5-hour test with 100 multiple-choice questions. You need to score at least 75% to pass. Each attempt requires a new application and a fee of $37.

  • Ownership of Property
  • Regulations and Land Use Controls
  • Market Analysis and Valuation
  • Real Estate Financing
  • Principles of Agency
  • Property Conditions and Required Disclosures
  • Contractual Agreements
  • Title Transfer Procedures
  • Real Estate Mathematical Calculations
  • Specialized Areas of Practice
  • Property Conditions and Required Disclosures
  • Contractual Agreements
  • Title Transfer Procedures
  • Real Estate Mathematical Calculations
  • Specialized Areas of Practice

Exam Preparation Tips

Go over the textbooks and notes from your pre-license courses. Revisiting these materials can reinforce your understanding of key concepts. Focus on areas where you feel less confident to ensure a well-rounded preparation.

Take as many practice exams as possible. This helps you get familiar with the exam format and identify areas where you need more study. Practice exams can also help you manage your time effectively during the actual test. (Free Michigan Real Estate Practice Exam)

Join or form study groups. Discussing topics with peers can enhance understanding and retention. Study groups provide a platform to share knowledge, ask questions, and support each other.

Consider enrolling in an exam prep course for additional support and structured study. These courses often include practice exams, study guides, and expert instruction. Investing in a prep course can increase your chances of passing the exam on the first attempt.

Exam Day Tips

Ensure you arrive at the exam center early to avoid any last-minute stress. Arriving early gives you time to settle in and focus before the exam begins. Check the location and travel time in advance to plan your journey.

You’ll need a valid photo ID to enter the exam. Make sure your ID is current and matches the name on your application. Having all necessary documents ready can prevent any issues at the exam center.

Read each question carefully and manage your time wisely. Staying calm and composed can help you think clearly and avoid mistakes. Take deep breaths and stay positive throughout the exam.

Follow all instructions given by the exam proctors. Adhering to the rules ensures a smooth and fair testing experience for everyone. If you have any questions or issues, ask the proctors for assistance.

After passing the exam, you must fulfill the fingerprinting requirement for a background check. This is a mandatory step to ensure you meet the honesty and integrity standards set by the Michigan Department of Licensing and Regulatory Affairs (LARA).

Use a Live Scan service provider approved by the Michigan State Police (MSP). Live Scan is an electronic fingerprinting process that is quick and secure. Locate a service provider near you and schedule an appointment (Costs typically range from $10 to $77).

Fill out and submit the required form along with the fingerprinting fee. The Live Scan service provider will electronically transmit your fingerprints to the MSP. Keep a copy of the form and receipt for your records.

Be prepared to pay the fingerprinting fee, which varies by provider. The fee generally includes the cost of the Live Scan service and the background check. Confirm the total cost when scheduling your appointment.

LARA will conduct a background check using your fingerprints. This check ensures you meet the honesty and integrity standards necessary for a real estate license in Michigan. Any issues identified during the background check may need to be addressed before your license is granted.

If any issues arise during the background check, you may need to provide additional documentation or explanations. Demonstrating honesty and taking responsibility for past actions can positively influence LARA’s decision. If necessary, seek legal advice to navigate any complex issues.

The next step is to submit your Salesperson License Application to confirm that you have passed the exam and completed the fingerprinting requirement.

Fill out the Salesperson License Application form available on the LARA website. Ensure all sections are accurately completed. You can access the form online.

Include proof that you passed the Michigan real estate exam. This documentation is essential for LARA to verify your eligibility. Keep a copy of your exam results for your records.

Attach your Live Scan Service form as proof that you have completed the fingerprinting requirement. Ensure all required signatures and details are included.

As of 2024, the fee for the Salesperson License in Michigan is $88. This fee must be submitted along with your application. Keep a copy of your payment receipt for future reference.

After submitting your application, you can track its status online through the LARA website. Regularly checking the status will keep you informed about any updates or additional requirements. If there are delays, contacting LARA directly can provide clarity and expedite the process.

Find a Sponsoring Broker

Once you pass the exam, you need to find a sponsoring broker to activate your license. Look for a brokerage that aligns with your goals and offers the support you need.

What to Look For in a Broker

Find a supportive and collaborative environment. The right culture can significantly impact your job satisfaction and success.

Understand the financial arrangements. Different brokerages offer different commission splits, so choose one that suits your financial goals.

Choose a broker who provides ample training and mentorship opportunities. Early career support is crucial for your development and success.

17 Brokerage Interview Questions

  • What training programs do you offer for new agents?
  • How does your commission split work, and are there any additional fees?
  • What kind of support and resources will I have access to?
  • Can you provide references from other agents in your brokerage?

Top Brokerages to Consider

Choosing the right broker is crucial as it impacts your early career significantly. Consider top-rated brokers like eXp Realty, Keller Williams, Coldwell Banker, and Compass.

Why Join Smart Agent Alliance?

When you join Smart Agent Alliance, you get double the value because you’re also automatically a Wolf Pack member.  Together, Smart Agent Alliance and the Wolf Pack provide the tools, support, and expertise to elevate your business to new heights.

Smart Agent Alliance Benefits

At Smart Agent Alliance, we prioritize organization, clarity, and efficiency to help our agents succeed. We provide essential onboarding resources, customizable presentations, business automations, client checklists, business assets, social media content, YouTube support, a growth plan, quality lead generation, production know-how, website support, and top-notch organizational tools.

Wolf Pack Benefits

The Wolf Pack team at eXp Realty offers expert-led courses like Social Agent Academy 3.0, the Wolf Pack Hub, and the Investor Army for property flipping. We provide tailored assistance for new agents, developing agents, top producers, team leaders, and broker owners. Key resources include lead generation, production know-how, social media content, and YouTube support.

Why eXp Realty?

eXp Realty offers a comprehensive range of benefits for agents, focusing on top pay structures, low fees, and free stock options. Agents get an 80/20 commission splits that cap at $16,000 annually, with opportunities to earn 100% commission, receive annual stock awards and gain income through Revenue Share. Additional resources include 24/7 agent support, affordable healthcare, live training, and advanced tech tools like kvCORE and customizable IDX websites.

Starting a career in real estate involves some initial costs and financial planning. Understanding these costs and planning accordingly will help you manage your finances effectively.

Initial Expenses Breakdown

All stated fees are subject to change.

  • Initial Expenses: These include licensing fees, marketing expenses, office supplies, and technology tools needed to start your career. For example, initial expenses may include:
    • Pre-Licensing Course: $100-$300+
    • Exam Fee: $79 per attempt
    • Application Fee: $88
    • Fingerprinting Fee: Roughly $10–$77
    • Marketing Materials: Business cards, signage, and online advertising costs
  • When you join a brokerage in Michigan, many will require you to become a member of the National, State, and Local Associations of Realtors. You’ll also need to budget for fees related to the Multiple Listing Service (MLS) and Supra Key services.
    • National Association of Realtors (NAR):
      • Membership Fees: For 2024, the annual dues for NAR are $156, plus a $45 special assessment fee for the Consumer Advertising Campaign, bringing the total to $201 per year.
    • Michigan Realtors (State Association):
      • Membership Fees: The annual dues for Michigan Realtors in 2024 are approximately $183. This fee is separate from the NAR dues.
    • Local Association of Realtors:
      • Membership Fees: Local association fees in Michigan can vary depending on the specific association. For example:
      • Greater Metropolitan Association of Realtors (GMAR): The annual dues are typically around $240 to $300.
      • Ann Arbor Area Board of Realtors (AAABoR): The fees usually range from $225 to $275 per year.
    • Multiple Listing Service (MLS):
      • Fees: MLS fees in Michigan also vary depending on the region. For instance:
        • Realcomp MLS, serving a large part of Michigan, charges approximately $396 annually.
        • Other local MLS services in Michigan might have similar fee structures.
    • Supra Key Fees:
      • Fees: Supra Key services in Michigan typically include an initial setup fee, around $50, and a monthly service fee ranging from $20 to $25.

These fees are approximate and may vary slightly depending on your specific location and the associations or MLS you join. It’s always best to confirm the exact fees with the local associations or MLS for the most accurate and up-to-date information.

  • Create a Budget: Track your expenses and forecast your income. Plan for slower months to ensure financial stability.
  • Emergency Fund: Set aside an emergency fund to cover unexpected expenses or slow periods in the market
  • Commission Structure: Real estate agents typically earn commissions from sales. Understand how your brokerage’s commission structure works and how to manage irregular income streams. Commission splits can vary widely, typically ranging from 50/50 to 70/30 or higher, depending on the brokerage.
  • Tax Planning: As an independent contractor, it’s important to plan for taxes. Keep track of deductible expenses, make estimated tax payments, and maintain good record-keeping practices. Consulting with a tax professional can help ensure compliance and optimize your tax strategy.

To get a Michigan real estate license, you must be at least 18 years old, complete 40 hours of pre-licensing education, pass the Michigan real estate exam, submit fingerprints for a background check, and file your application with LARA along with the $88 fee.

The cost to get a real estate license in Florida typically ranges from $377 to $544+. This includes the pre-licensing course ($200-$300), application fee ($88), background check fee ($10-$77), and exam fee ($79)

It typically takes 3 to 6 months to get a real estate license in Michigan. This timeframe includes completing the 40-hour pre-licensing course, passing the exam, submitting fingerprints, and waiting for the Michigan Department of Licensing and Regulatory Affairs (LARA) to process your application.

The pass rate for the Michigan real estate exam varies, but generally, it is around 58-64%, depending on the source and the exam preparation provider. For example, some providers report a pass rate of 64%, while others have lower rates for different portions of the exam.

What happens if I fail the real estate exam?

If you fail the Michigan real estate exam, you can retake it. You’ll need to reschedule the exam and pay the exam fee again. There is no limit to the number of times you can retake the exam, but it’s essential to review the areas where you struggled before attempting the test again.

To renew your Michigan real estate license, complete 18 hours of continuing education, including 2 hours of legal updates each year. Then, submit the renewal application and pay the fee online through the LARA website before your license expires.

content:
Choosing one of the best online Michigan real estate school is crucial for your career, and it’s important to thoroughly research your options and understand what each course offers. While there are countless real estate schools available, only a select few stand out for their exceptional education and comprehensive support in preparing you to obtain your real estate license.

At Smart Agent Alliance, we are committed to guiding new agents in launching successful careers. That’s why we’ve curated a list of the leading online real estate schools in Michigan. Our detailed analysis covers the offerings of each school, their course delivery methods, and a balanced view of the advantages and disadvantages of studying with each one.

Table of Contents

  1. AceableAgent
  2. The CE Shop
  3. Colibri Real Estate
  4. RealEstateU

AceableAgent

SmartAgentAlliance Score: A+
Top Feature: Ability to study on the go with the mobile app

Why We Chose It:

AceableAgent has quickly become a favorite among students due to its modern approach and flexibility. It is exclusively focused on real estate education, offering courses for every step of a real estate career, from pre-licensing to continuing education and career advancement. The courses are state-approved and designed to be accessible from any device, providing students with the flexibility to study anywhere.

Overview:

AceableAgent offers three pre-licensing packages, all of which include the required coursework. The courses are fully online and accessible via desktop, laptop, tablet, or smartphone. This accessibility is one of AceableAgent’s standout features, allowing students to study on the go. Courses are developed by experienced professionals and are accredited by ARELLO. Additionally, AceableAgent is known for its interactive coursework, which helps students grasp and retain information effectively.

Why AceableAgent is Great for Pre-Licensing:

AceableAgent’s pre-licensing courses are available in multiple states, ensuring they meet state-specific requirements. The courses include engaging video lectures, interactive content, and practice exams to help students prepare effectively for their licensing exams. AceableAgent offers a satisfaction guarantee and provides career resources to help new agents start their careers successfully.

User Experiences and Feedback:

Students have reported that AceableAgent’s courses are particularly user-friendly, with a modern interface that makes navigation easy. The mobile app is highly rated for its convenience, allowing users to study during commutes or breaks. The interactive content, including quizzes and video explanations, has been praised for making complex topics easier to understand. Additionally, AceableAgent’s customer service, though sometimes slow, is generally helpful and responsive to student needs.

Pre-Licensing

Price Starts at: $169
Basic Package Features: The AceableAgent 40-Hour Course includes instructor support via email, Lite Exam Prep with 1,000 practice questions, a mini course on finding a sponsoring broker, and fully narrated courses with an audio guide for all lessons.
Upgrade Packages: Yes, the upgraded packages include The Virtual Instructor package which offers 24/7 AI-assisted learning, 3 months of PrepAgent State Exam Prep with 75 videos, 70 audio lessons, digital flashcards, and 1,660+ practice questions. It includes a real estate exam prep ebook, 1 hour of private tutoring, and live webinars with Q&A sessions held five times a week.

Exam Prep

Price Starts at: $59
Basic Package Features: Access to Prep Agent with Thousands of practice questions; 100% money-back guarantee; 75+ real estate exam prep videos; online interactive flashcards; 100+ page exam prep ebook; hours of audio lessons; interactive vocabulary worksheet; 100s of hours of recorded webinars; live online webinars.
Upgrade Packages: Yes, the upgraded packages offer access to PrepAgent for 1 or 3 months, compared to just 1 week with the basic plan.

Flexible Learning Options: Enjoy self-paced courses that fit your schedule.

Extended Course Access: Access course materials for up to six months from your enrollment date.

Risk-Free Enrollment: Benefit from a 30-day money-back guarantee if you’re not satisfied.

Exam Success Guarantee: Pass the licensing exam within three tries or receive a full course refund.

Comprehensive Exam Preparation: Gain access to a variety of study tools including prep videos, 75 audio lessons, digital flashcards, over 1600 practice questions, a 100-page exam prep ebook, weekly live webinars, live Q&A sessions with instructors, and the option for personalized 1-on-1 tutoring.

Dedicated Support: Instructor assistance is available seven days a week via email, and customer service can be reached by email and phone from Monday to Friday.

Convenient Final Exams: Utilize online proctoring services for the final exam across the nation, although California students do not need a proctor for their pre-licensing exam.

Mobile-Friendly Platform: Study anytime, anywhere with a course platform that is fully optimized for mobile devices.

Interactive Learning Tools: Engage with interactive content designed to enhance your learning experience and retention.

Career Resources: Access additional career support services to help you transition from student to professional agent successfully.

Pros:

  • Mobile app for studying
  • High pass rates among schools with 1,000+ students
  • Engaging and entertaining course materials
  • Exam prep through PrepAgent

Cons:

  • Course access lasts for 12 months
  • Some bugs reported in the mobile app
  • Limited technical support
  • Outsourced exam proctoring

To view their current pricing for pre-licensing click this linked button. If you are interested in any of the other courses they offer, you can find that through this link as well.

The CE Shop

SmartAgentAlliance Score: A
Top Feature: Course is easy to follow, high pass rates among students

Why We Chose It: The CE Shop offers a variety of real estate education options with an interactive and inquiry-based approach in their studying methods. While their focus is mainly on continuing education, they also provide excellent pre-licensing courses, exam prep, and post-licensing education. Their courses are accredited by ARELLO and have received numerous awards for their educational programs.

Overview: The CE Shop’s pre-licensing courses can be taken in 34 states, through four different course packages: Courses Only, Standard Package, Value Package, and Premium Package. Every package comes with business ebooks, career resources, downloadable resources, real estate glossary, digital flashcards, study schedules, and access to instructor-led webinars. Students have praised the course content for being easy to follow and highly informative, which has led to high pass rates.

Why The CE Shop is Great for Pre-Licensing: The CE Shop’s pre-licensing courses emphasize real-world applications with scenario-based learning. This approach helps students apply their knowledge to practical situations they will face in their real estate careers. Additionally, The CE Shop provides an exam prep edge program that offers additional practice tests and study aids to ensure students are well-prepared for their licensing exams.

User Experiences and Feedback: The CE Shop is well-regarded for its robust customer support and comprehensive resources. The ability to access instructor-led webinars provides an additional layer of support and interaction, which can be particularly beneficial for students who need more guidance. The frequent promotions and discounts make it a cost-effective option for many. Students appreciate the clear and structured study schedules, which help them stay on track and complete the course efficiently.

Pre-Licensing

Price Starts at: $156.75
Basic Package Features: Includes all 40 mandatory hours required for the Michigan Real Estate Salesperson license. It prepares you for the state licensing exam and equips you with essential business knowledge for a successful real estate career in Michigan.
Upgrade Packages: Includes the 40-hour MI Fundamentals of Real Estate for Salespersons Course, the eTextbook “Real Estate Basics and Beyond,” and MI Exam Prep Edge, fulfilling the requirements for a Michigan Salesperson license. It also includes your first license renewal package for free and the ProPath Real Estate Career Kickstarter, offering 9 hours of professional development with courses on business planning, lead generation, and negotiation skills.

Exam Prep

Price Starts at: $101.25
Basic Package Features: Fully prepares you for the Michigan Salesperson national and state licensing exams using Exam Prep Edge, an intuitive online study tool. It includes an initial assessment with instant feedback, dynamic practice exams, and real-time dashboards to track your progress and focus on areas needing improvement before exam day.
Upgrade Packages: None

Continuing Education

Price Starts at: $89

title: Sotheby’s vs Berkshire Hathaway: Which is Best for Realtors?
excerpt: This article will provide a comprehensive comparison between Sotheby’s and Berkshire Hathaway to help you determine which brokerage better aligns with your professional goals. Choosing…
content:
Sotheby’s International Realty and Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices are two of the most prestigious names in luxury real estate. Both attract agents who work in high-end markets, both leverage powerful parent brands, and both charge premium fees for the privilege of carrying their name. But the brands operate differently, and the costs are structured in ways that can significantly impact your bottom line.

Sotheby’s trades on 280 years of auction house heritage and global luxury recognition. Berkshire Hathaway trades on Warren Buffett’s reputation for financial integrity and stability. Both names open doors in luxury markets – but with different clients and in different ways.

This comparison breaks down the real numbers on commissions, fees, and total annual costs alongside the differences in training, technology, culture, and support that determine which brand is the right fit for your luxury real estate career.

  • Commission Structure
  • Total Annual Cost at Different Production Levels
  • Training and Professional Development
  • Technology and Tools
  • Culture and Work Environment
  • Brand Recognition and Market Presence
  • Agent Support
  • Who Should Choose Sotheby’s International Realty
  • Who Should Choose Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices
  • The Bottom Line
  • Frequently Asked Questions

Commission Structure

Both Sotheby’s and Berkshire Hathaway operate as franchise models where individual offices set their own commission structures. This means the numbers below represent typical ranges – your actual split, fees, and costs depend entirely on the specific office you join. What’s consistent across both brands is that neither offers a commission cap, and both charge franchise royalty fees on top of the commission split.

Sotheby’s International Realty Commission Structure

Sotheby’s positions itself at the top of the luxury market, and its fee structure reflects that positioning. The combination of a royalty fee plus a separate advertising fee creates a higher total franchise burden than most competitors.

  • Commission split: 70/30 to 90/10 (varies by office, production level, and negotiation)
  • Royalty fee: 6% of gross commission per transaction
  • Advertising fee: 2% of gross commission per transaction (separate from royalty)
  • Total franchise fee: 8% combined (royalty + advertising)
  • Commission cap: No (some offices may cap at approximately $18,000, but this is not standard)
  • Monthly fees: Varies by office ($62.50 – $292.50/month reported)
  • Transaction fees: Included in the 6% + 2% royalty/advertising structure
  • E&O insurance: ~$2,200/year

The 8% combined franchise fee is the headline number. On a $20,000 commission, $1,600 goes to the franchise before the office even takes its split. This adds up fast, especially with no cap to limit total annual costs. Luxury agents dealing with large commissions feel this acutely – an 8% royalty on a $100,000 commission is $8,000.

Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices Commission Structure

Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices has a similar franchise structure but with a slightly lower total franchise fee and its own set of variable costs.

  • Commission split: 60/40 to 90/10 (varies by office, production level, and negotiation)
  • Royalty fee: 6-7% of gross commission (declining structure at some offices)
  • Commission cap: No (rare office-level exceptions)
  • Monthly fees: Varies by office ($98 – $140/month reported)
  • Transaction fees: $295 – $625 per transaction
  • E&O insurance: Varies by office (agent responsibility)

BHHS doesn’t charge a separate advertising fee like Sotheby’s, but it does charge per-transaction fees ($295 – $625) that Sotheby’s folds into its royalty structure. The net effect is that BHHS’s total franchise burden is slightly lower than Sotheby’s – typically 6-7% royalty versus Sotheby’s 8% combined – but the starting split can be worse (60/40 at the low end versus Sotheby’s 70/30).

Total Annual Cost at Different Production Levels

Both brokerages charge premium fees with no commission cap. This makes total annual costs scale linearly with production – the more you produce, the more you pay in absolute dollars. Here’s how the numbers compare at three production levels.

Sotheby’s International Realty Annual Cost Estimates

Estimates assume 75/25 split (mid-range), 5 deals at $100K GCI, 10 deals at $250K GCI, 20 deals at $500K GCI (luxury volume, larger average deal size). Monthly fees averaged at $175/mo. Actual costs vary significantly by office.

Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices Annual Cost Estimates

Estimates assume 70/30 split, 5 deals at $100K GCI, 10 deals at $250K GCI, 20 deals at $500K GCI, with average $450 transaction fee. Actual costs vary significantly by office.

Head-to-Head: $250K GCI Comparison

At $250,000 in gross commission income:

  • Sotheby’s International Realty: ~$86,800 in total costs – you keep ~$163,200 (65%)
  • Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices: ~$98,340 in total costs – you keep ~$151,660 (61%)

Sotheby’s comes out about $11,500 cheaper at $250K GCI, primarily because of a better starting split (75/25 vs 70/30 in these estimates). But this gap is heavily dependent on the specific splits you negotiate at each brokerage. An agent who negotiates an 80/20 split at BHHS would beat the Sotheby’s numbers.

The larger takeaway is that both brokerages are expensive compared to capped or flat-fee alternatives. Neither caps commissions, so agents at both brands pay their brokerage a percentage on every deal, all year long. An agent doing $500K GCI pays roughly $169,000 – $193,000 in total brokerage costs. At a capped brokerage, those same costs might be $30,000 – $50,000.

The question is whether the luxury brand premium justifies the cost difference. If the Sotheby’s or BHHS name helps you win listings you wouldn’t otherwise get, the higher fees are a business expense that generates ROI. If you’d close the same deals regardless of the sign in the yard, you’re paying for a brand you don’t need.

Training and Professional Development

Sotheby’s International Realty Training

Sotheby’s training is limited at the corporate level. The brand provides some educational resources and the prestige of association with the Sotheby’s auction house, but it does not have a signature training program comparable to what some larger brokerages offer.

  • Onboarding: Varies significantly by franchise office
  • Corporate resources: Some brand-level educational materials and luxury market positioning guides
  • Luxury-specific training: Positioning, marketing to high-net-worth clients, and working with international buyers
  • Mentorship: Dependent on office culture and individual broker relationships

Sotheby’s training experience depends almost entirely on which office you join. Some offices have seasoned managing brokers who mentor agents extensively. Others expect you to arrive as a polished professional who doesn’t need training. If training quality is important to you, ask detailed questions about the specific office’s program before joining.

Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices Training

BHHS provides training through its Career Development Department, but like Sotheby’s, the depth and quality varies by franchise location.

  • Career Development Department: Corporate-level training resources and programs
  • Onboarding: Varies by office, some have structured programs with mentorship
  • Continuing education: Access to corporate educational materials
  • Office-level training: Dependent on local franchise leadership

BHHS has a slight edge over Sotheby’s in training consistency because the Career Development Department provides a baseline level of corporate support. But neither brokerage is a training-first company. Both attract experienced agents who already know how to sell, not newcomers who need to learn the basics. If you need substantial training to succeed, neither of these luxury brands is the right fit.

Technology and Tools

Sotheby’s International Realty Technology

Sotheby’s provides technology tools focused on luxury marketing and global exposure:

  • SothebysRealty.com: Global listing platform with premium presentation
  • Marketing suite: High-end branded materials, professional templates, and digital marketing tools
  • Global network: Listing syndication across international markets and the broader Sotheby’s ecosystem
  • Video and media: Access to the Sotheby’s International Realty YouTube channel and video marketing resources

Sotheby’s technology strength is in presentation rather than lead generation or CRM. The listing platform is beautifully designed and projects the luxury positioning the brand is known for. For agents marketing $5M+ properties to international buyers, the global exposure matters.

Most Sotheby’s agents supplement corporate tools with their own CRM, lead generation, and marketing technology.

Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices Technology

BHHS provides a functional technology suite leveraging the brand’s corporate resources:

  • REsides: CRM and transaction management platform
  • BHHSagent.com: Agent website platform
  • Marketing tools: Branded templates and materials
  • Listing syndication: Distribution to major real estate portals

BHHS’s technology is functional and professional but not cutting-edge. The tools get the job done and maintain the brand’s polished image. Like Sotheby’s, many BHHS agents use third-party tools for CRM and lead generation rather than relying exclusively on corporate technology.

Neither brokerage differentiates on technology. Both provide the basic tools you need with premium branding, and both expect that serious luxury agents will invest in their own technology stack. This is consistent with the premium pricing model – you’re paying for the brand, not the tech.

Culture and Work Environment

Sotheby’s International Realty Culture

Sotheby’s culture is defined by luxury, exclusivity, and global sophistication. The brand attracts agents who operate in the upper echelons of the real estate market and who value prestige as a core part of their professional identity.

  • Elite, exclusive atmosphere focused on luxury properties and high-net-worth clients
  • International orientation – the Sotheby’s brand resonates globally
  • Emphasis on discretion, professionalism, and white-glove service
  • Smaller office environments with selective agent recruitment

Sotheby’s offices tend to be smaller and more curated than the typical BHHS office. The brand is intentionally selective about who carries the Sotheby’s name, which creates an exclusive feel. This works well for agents who want to be part of a boutique, high-end environment rather than a large, bustling office.

Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices Culture

BHHS culture centers on trust, stability, and the Buffett legacy. It’s premium without being as exclusive as Sotheby’s, attracting agents across a broader range of the luxury market.

  • Professional, polished environment associated with financial stability
  • Trust-driven brand that appeals to agents who value long-term relationships
  • Broader agent base than Sotheby’s – not exclusively luxury
  • Traditional office settings with established, collegial relationships

BHHS offices generally have more agents and a wider range of price points than Sotheby’s offices. While the brand positions itself as premium, many BHHS agents work in the $500K – $2M range rather than exclusively ultra-luxury. This creates a more diverse and less rarefied culture than Sotheby’s.

The cultural difference is real: Sotheby’s feels like a private club. BHHS feels like a well-run, professional firm. Both are premium, but in different ways.

Brand Recognition and Market Presence

Sotheby’s International Realty Brand

The Sotheby’s name carries 280 years of history. The auction house is synonymous with the finest art, collectibles, and luxury goods in the world. That brand equity transfers to the real estate division in a way that’s difficult for any competitor to replicate.

Sotheby’s International Realty has approximately 26,000 agents across 1,100 offices in 83 countries. The global footprint is a genuine competitive advantage for agents who work with international buyers and sellers. A $10M listing on SothebysRealty.com gets exposure to affluent buyers across Asia, Europe, the Middle East, and the Americas.

In the ultra-luxury segment ($5M+), the Sotheby’s name may be the most recognizable real estate brand on the planet. Below that threshold, the name recognition narrows.

Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices Brand

The Berkshire Hathaway name is one of the most trusted brands in American business. Warren Buffett’s reputation for integrity, financial acumen, and long-term thinking lends the real estate division a credibility that resonates strongly with domestic buyers and sellers.

BHHS has approximately 55,000+ agents across 1,500+ offices, with a presence primarily in the US. The domestic footprint is larger than Sotheby’s, and the brand recognition is broader among American consumers. The burgundy and cream color scheme is instantly recognizable.

Where Sotheby’s has the global luxury edge, BHHS has the domestic trust edge. In the US market, the Berkshire Hathaway name signals financial stability and trustworthiness in a way that resonates with both luxury and upper-middle-market clients. Internationally, the brand doesn’t carry the same weight as Sotheby’s.

Agent Support

Sotheby’s International Realty Agent Support

Agent support at Sotheby’s is office-dependent and typically more personalized due to smaller office sizes. You’re likely to have more direct access to your managing broker simply because there are fewer agents in the office.

  • 24/7 support: No
  • Managing broker access: Generally more accessible due to smaller office size
  • Marketing support: Corporate brand resources for luxury marketing
  • Global referral network: International referral capabilities through the SIR network

The global referral network is a meaningful support feature for agents who deal with cross-border clients. Being able to refer (or receive referrals from) Sotheby’s agents in 83 countries adds a dimension of support that BHHS can’t fully match.

Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices Agent Support

BHHS support follows the standard franchise model – quality depends on your specific office.

  • 24/7 support: No
  • Managing broker access: Varies by office size and leadership
  • Career Development Department: Corporate-level support resources
  • Transaction support: Some offices provide dedicated transaction coordinators

Neither brokerage offers 24/7 support. Both provide business-hours support through office staff and managing brokers. The support experience at both brands comes down to the specific office you join, which makes due diligence before committing essential.

Who Should Choose Sotheby’s International Realty

Sotheby’s International Realty is the stronger choice if you:

  • Work in ultra-luxury markets ($5M+) where the Sotheby’s brand carries unique prestige and global recognition
  • Serve international clients and need the global network of 1,100 offices in 83 countries for referrals and exposure
  • Value exclusivity and want to be part of a curated, boutique office environment
  • Market to high-net-worth individuals who associate the Sotheby’s name with the auction house’s 280-year legacy of luxury
  • Sell properties that benefit from global exposure on SothebysRealty.com
  • Already have an established luxury practice and don’t need extensive training or hand-holding

Sotheby’s delivers the most value to agents in the true luxury segment where the brand name opens doors that other names cannot. If your average sale price is under $1M, you’re paying a premium for brand equity that won’t materially impact your business.

Who Should Choose Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices

Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices is the stronger choice if you:

  • Work primarily in the US market where the Buffett brand carries significant trust and recognition
  • Serve the broader luxury and upper-middle market ($500K – $5M) where trust and stability matter more than ultra-luxury cachet
  • Want a larger domestic network – BHHS has more offices and agents in the US than Sotheby’s
  • Value the stability signal that Warren Buffett’s name sends to clients – financial integrity and long-term thinking
  • Prefer a professional but less rarefied environment with a broader range of agents and price points
  • Want the Career Development Department for ongoing education and corporate support

BHHS works best for agents who need a premium brand but don’t exclusively operate in the ultra-luxury tier. The Berkshire Hathaway name impresses clients across a wide range of price points and market segments within the US.

The Bottom Line

This is a comparison between two premium brands that charge premium fees. Neither caps commissions. Neither offers revenue sharing or profit sharing. Neither has standout training or technology. You’re paying for a name – and both names are exceptional.

Sotheby’s is the global luxury brand. If you sell $5M+ properties to international buyers, the Sotheby’s name carries weight that no other real estate brand can match. The 280-year auction house legacy, the global network in 83 countries, and the exclusive, curated office culture position you at the very top of the luxury market.

Berkshire Hathaway is the American trust brand. If you sell premium properties to domestic clients in the $500K – $5M range, the Buffett name signals integrity, financial stability, and long-term thinking in ways that resonate deeply with American buyers and sellers.

At $250K GCI, Sotheby’s is roughly $11,500 cheaper in this comparison, but that gap depends heavily on the specific splits negotiated at each office. Both brokerages take 35-40% of your GCI when you add up splits, royalties, and fees. That’s a significant cost that only makes sense if the brand is actively helping you win business you wouldn’t get otherwise.

Be honest about your market and your clients. If the brand opens doors, it’s worth the premium. If you’d close the same deals with any yard sign, you’re overpaying for prestige.

Frequently Asked Questions

Does Sotheby’s International Realty have a commission cap?

Generally, no. Some individual Sotheby’s offices may cap at approximately $18,000, but this is not standard across the brand. Most Sotheby’s offices charge the commission split plus the 8% combined royalty/advertising fee on every transaction throughout the year. This no-cap structure is one of the most significant cost factors for high-producing agents.

Which brand is better known globally?

Sotheby’s has stronger global recognition, particularly in the luxury segment. The 280-year auction house heritage and presence in 83 countries give Sotheby’s International Realty international brand equity that BHHS doesn’t match. Within the United States, both brands are well-known, but BHHS benefits from the Warren Buffett association, which is the most recognized business brand in America. For international transactions, Sotheby’s wins. For domestic transactions, it’s roughly equal.

Do either brokerage offer revenue sharing or passive income?

No, neither Sotheby’s International Realty nor Berkshire Hathaway HomeServices offers revenue sharing, profit sharing, or any form of passive income tied to recruiting other agents. Your income at both brokerages is entirely based on your own production. This is one area where both luxury brands differ significantly from brokerages like eXp Realty, Keller Williams, or LPT Realty that offer agent-to-agent revenue or profit sharing programs.

Which brokerage is better for new agents?

Neither is ideal for new agents. Both Sotheby’s and BHHS target experienced professionals who already have established practices and clientele. The fee structures are expensive for agents still building their business, and neither brand provides the kind of intensive, structured training that new agents need. A new agent would be better served starting at a brokerage with stronger training programs and lower costs, then potentially moving to a luxury brand once they’ve established their business.

What’s the actual difference in total annual cost between the two?

At $250K GCI with mid-range splits, Sotheby’s costs roughly $86,800 versus BHHS at approximately $98,340 – a difference of about $11,500 favoring Sotheby’s. However, this gap is highly sensitive to the specific splits you negotiate. If you get an 80/20 split at BHHS versus 75/25 at Sotheby’s, the numbers shift considerably. Always calculate your total cost based on the actual terms offered by the specific office you’re considering, not brand-level averages.

Can you negotiate the royalty and advertising fees?

The commission split is negotiable at both brokerages, but the franchise royalty and advertising fees are generally set by the franchisor and not negotiable at the individual office level. Sotheby’s 6% royalty + 2% advertising fee and BHHS’s 6-7% royalty are corporate requirements that your local office passes through. Some high-producing agents at large offices may have leverage to negotiate how these fees are applied, but this is the exception rather than the rule.

title: How to Get a Real Estate License in Illinois: 2026 Guide
excerpt: Embarking on the journey to obtain your Illinois real estate license might seem overwhelming, but with the proper guidance, it becomes a manageable process. This…
content:
Illinois does real estate licensing a little differently than most states. What other states call a “salesperson” or “agent” license, Illinois calls a Broker license. Don’t let the name confuse you – it’s the entry-level license. What other states call a “broker,” Illinois calls a Managing Broker. With that cleared up, here’s how to get licensed.

Quick Overview

Age: 18+ | Education: 75 hours | Exam: 140 questions, 4 hours, 70-75% to pass | Total cost: ~$560-$1,000

What You Need Before You Start

You need to be at least 18 years old, have a high school diploma or GED, and hold a Social Security number or ITIN. No Illinois residency is required.

IDFPR (Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation) runs a background check through electronic fingerprinting. A criminal history doesn’t automatically disqualify you, but it will be reviewed before a license is issued.

New in 2026

Illinois now uses an Endorsement process instead of reciprocity for out-of-state licensees. If you’re licensed in another state, you’ll need to complete a 30-hour Illinois Endorsement course and pass the state portion of the exam.

Step 1: Complete 75 Hours of Pre-Licensing Education

Illinois requires 75 hours split into two parts:

  • Real Estate Broker Topics (60 hours) – License law, agency, contracts, property ownership, valuation, fair housing, financing, environmental issues, closing procedures, and property management. This can be done online self-paced, classroom, or live webinar.
  • Applied Real Estate Principles (15 hours) – The hands-on portion. This must be completed live – either in-person classroom or live interactive webinar. Online self-paced is not allowed for this section.

Budget $300-$700 depending on the school. Popular IDFPR-approved providers include Colibri Real Estate, Kaplan, AceableAgent, and the Illinois REALTORS education programs.

Don’t Miss This

The 15-hour Applied Real Estate Principles section cannot be self-paced online. You must attend either a physical classroom or a live webinar with real-time interaction. Plan your schedule around this requirement.

Step 2: Apply and Get Fingerprinted

Submit your application through the IDFPR online portal. The Broker license application fee is $150 (increased January 2024).

You’ll need electronic fingerprinting from an IDFPR-licensed vendor. Cost is typically $50-$75. Fingerprints must be taken within 60 days of submitting your application.

Results go to the Illinois State Police and are forwarded to IDFPR. Processing takes a few weeks.

Step 3: Pass the Illinois Real Estate Exam

The exam is administered by PSI Exams at testing centers across Illinois (Chicago, Schaumburg, Springfield, Rockford, Champaign, and more) or via remote online proctoring from home.

Exam Format

National: 100 questions, 2.5 hours, 70% to pass | State: 40 questions, 90 min, 75% to pass | Fee: $58/attempt | Schedule at psiexams.com

You must pass both sections. If you fail one, you only retake that section. The state portion has a higher passing threshold (75% vs. 70%) and tends to trip up students who under-prepare on Illinois-specific law.

How to Prepare

The first-time pass rate in Illinois is around 67-75% depending on the source, which is better than states like Florida or Texas. Still, roughly a third of first-time takers fail at least one section.

  • Master the state portion. Illinois license law, IDFPR regulations, and the Real Estate License Act are heavily tested. This is where most failures happen.
  • Understand the terminology. Remember – Illinois uses “Broker” where other states say “agent” and “Managing Broker” where others say “broker.” The exam will use Illinois terminology.
  • Take practice exams. Free resources: Real Estate License Wizard, AceableAgent, OpenExamPrep (500+ free questions with AI tutor)
  • Use flashcards for state-specific terms. Quizlet has Illinois state-focused flashcards.

If You Don’t Pass

You get 4 total attempts within 2 years of completing your education. Each retake costs $58, with a 24-hour wait between attempts. You only retake the section you failed. If you fail all 4 attempts, you must redo the entire 75-hour pre-licensing coursework before trying again.

Step 4: Find a Sponsoring Managing Broker

You cannot practice without a sponsoring Managing Broker. In Illinois, this is the equivalent of what other states call a “sponsoring broker.”

45-Day Head Start

Illinois allows newly licensed Brokers to start working under their Managing Broker for up to 45 days while their license application is being processed. You don’t have to wait for the paperwork to clear before getting started.

What to Look for in a Brokerage

  • Post-license education support. Illinois requires 45 hours of post-license education before your first renewal. A good brokerage helps you get through this efficiently.
  • Training and mentorship. Your Managing Broker is required to oversee your earnest money handling, contract negotiations, and advertising until you complete post-license education. Choose someone who takes this role seriously.
  • Commission structure. Understand the full cost: split, cap, desk fees, transaction fees, monthly fees. Compare total annual costs across brokerages.
  • Technology and tools. CRM, marketing, lead generation, transaction management. Ask what’s included versus what’s extra.

Want to see how different brokerage models compare? Check out our team value breakdown or browse our eXp Realty guides to see one example of a cloud-based model.

Step 5: Complete Post-License Education

Before your first renewal (April 30 of the next even-numbered year), you must complete 45 hours of post-license education in three 15-hour courses:

  1. Applied Brokerage Principles
  2. Risk Management and Discipline (includes Sexual Harassment Prevention)
  3. Transactional Issues

After your first renewal, continuing education drops to 12 hours every two years (6-hour Core course including Fair Housing + 6 hours of electives including Sexual Harassment Prevention). Renewal fee is $200.

Total Costs Breakdown

What You’ll Spend

Pre-licensing courses (75 hrs): $300-$700 | Application: $150 | Exam: $58 | Fingerprinting: $50-$75 | Total: ~$560-$985

Illinois falls in the mid-range for total cost. The application fee increased in January 2024 from the previous amount to $150. Your license renews every two years for $200.

How Long Does It Take?

  • Pre-licensing education (75 hrs): 3-6 weeks
  • Fingerprinting + background check: 3-6 weeks
  • Exam prep + testing: 1-3 weeks
  • Application processing: 1-4 business days

Most people complete the process in 2-4 months. If you go full-time and have a Managing Broker lined up, you could be working in as little as 4-6 weeks thanks to the 45-day working provision.

Common Questions

Why does Illinois call it a “Broker” license?

Illinois eliminated the “salesperson” license category. The entry-level license is now called Broker. The supervisory license is Managing Broker. The terminology is different but the function is the same as agent/broker in other states.

Can I take the exam from home?

Yes. PSI offers remote online proctoring. You need a webcam, microphone, stable internet, and a private quiet room. The exam content is identical to the in-person version.

Is the exam really hard?

Illinois has a better pass rate than many states (67-75% first-time). The state portion is tougher than the national portion. Students who study Illinois-specific law thoroughly tend to pass on their first try.

What if I only fail one section?

You only retake the section you failed. You have 4 total attempts within 2 years. Each retake is $58.

I have a license in another state. Can I transfer it?

Illinois now uses an Endorsement process (as of 2026). You’ll need to complete a 30-hour Illinois Endorsement course, pass the state portion of the exam, and submit an endorsement application with proof of your current out-of-state license.

Our Review Process:

At SmartAgentAlliance, our goal is to provide accurate and helpful reviews of online real estate schools to assist prospective real estate agents in making informed decisions. Our review process involves several key steps:

Research and Data Collection:

We begin by gathering comprehensive information about each real estate school, including course offerings, pricing, accreditation, and user reviews. This involves visiting the schools’ websites, reading third-party reviews, and gathering feedback from current and former students.

Evaluation Criteria:

Our evaluation is based on several important criteria, including:

  • Course Quality: We assess the content and structure of the courses to ensure they cover all necessary topics comprehensively and are easy to follow.
  • Accessibility: We evaluate the flexibility and accessibility of the courses, including the availability of mobile apps and study materials.
  • Pass Rates: We consider the pass rates of students who have completed the courses and taken the licensing exam.
  • Student Support: We look at the level of support provided to students, including access to instructors, customer service, and additional resources like study guides and practice exams.
  • Pricing: We compare the cost of the courses relative to the value they offer, including any discounts or payment plans available.
  • User Feedback: We gather and analyze feedback from students who have taken the courses to get an understanding of their experiences and satisfaction levels.

Scoring and Ranking:

Each school is scored based on the evaluation criteria, and an overall score is calculated. The scores are then used to rank the schools, with additional considerations given to standout features that may set a school apart from the rest.

The CE Shop, Colibri Real Estate Schools, and 360 Training.

The Illinois real estate pre-license courses cover essential topics to prepare you for the state licensing exam and a successful career in real estate. Key areas include:

  1. Real Estate Principles: Basic concepts, property rights, and ownership types.
  2. Illinois Real Estate Law: Contracts, agency relationships, and fair housing.
  3. Finance: Mortgages, financing, and lender roles.
  4. Property Valuation: Market analysis and appraisal techniques.
  5. Real Estate Practice: Listing, marketing, negotiations, and closings.
  6. Ethics: Professional standards and conduct.
  7. Agency & Brokerage: Broker-agent relationships and fiduciary duties.
  8. Math Skills: Calculating commissions, property taxes, and mortgages.
  9. Exam Prep: Practice questions and test strategies.

Completing these courses equips you with the knowledge to pass the Illinois licensing exam and succeed in the real estate market.

When choosing the best online Illinois real estate school, consider:

  1. Accreditation: Ensure IDFPR approval.
  2. Content: Comprehensive, Illinois-specific curriculum.
  3. Flexibility: Self-paced or live options.
  4. Support: Access to instructors and study aids.
  5. Reputation: Positive reviews and high pass rates.
  6. Cost: Affordable without sacrificing quality.
